planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; grass-leaved ladies' tresses thrives in wet, open pine savannas, wet meadows, and roadside ditches of the coastal plain — purchase only nursery-propagated stock.

directions: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in spring in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; grass-leaved ladies' tresses grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, open bogs, moist meadows, and roadside wet ditches throughout the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Texas, often in large populations. Spiranthes praecox is one of the more unusual ladies' tresses species, distinctive for its very narrow, grass-like basal leaves and its relatively early bloom time — the tightly spiraled, pure-white flower spikes appear from March through May, earlier than most other Spiranthes, making it an important early-season nectar source for native bees emerging in spring. It is much easier to cultivate than most native orchids as long as the soil remains consistently moist and acidic; plant at the margin of a rain garden, bog garden, or retention pond. Never collect from the wild; purchase nursery-propagated plants from reputable native plant nurseries.

planting.note: Plant with surrounding undisturbed Sphagnum or forest soil in spring in moist, strongly acidic, cold, boreal or montane woodland or bog conditions in partial to full shade; heart-leaved twayblade is a tiny circumboreal orchid that requires undisturbed mycorrhizal soil and cool, acidic conditions — strictly a plant for specialized woodland or bog gardens in cold climates.

directions: Plant only with surrounding undisturbed Sphagnum or forest duff soil in spring in moist, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0), cold, boreal or subalpine woodland or bog conditions in full shade; heart-leaved twayblade is extremely sensitive to disturbance and requires specific mycorrhizal fungal associations to survive. Neottia cordata (formerly Listera cordata) is a minute, circumboreal terrestrial orchid of cold, acid, Sphagnum-dominated bogs, conifer forests, and mountain stream banks throughout the Northern Hemisphere; despite its tiny size — the entire plant is 4–8 inches tall — it is a gem of botanical detail, with just two small, opposite, heart-shaped leaves at mid-stem and a slender, loose spike of 6–15 tiny, dark reddish-purple flowers with a deeply forked lip. It is extremely challenging to cultivate outside of Sphagnum bog settings and is recommended only for specialists with bog gardens or cold, moist, acidic woodland conditions. Never collect from the wild. A botanical treasure for naturalistic woodland garden enthusiasts in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to moderately moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, rocky or gravelly woodland soil in partial to full shade; limestone oak fern is one of the few ferns that prefers alkaline conditions and grows naturally in shaded limestone cliff crevices and rocky calcareous woods.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, rocky or gravelly, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; limestone oak fern is one of the few ferns with a preference for calcareous (limestone-rich) substrates, making it valuable for alkaline woodland gardens where most ferns struggle. Gymnocarpium robertianum is a delicate, graceful fern of shaded limestone cliffs, rocky calcareous woods, and talus slopes in northern North America and Europe, producing small (6–15 inch), broadly triangular, three-pinnate fronds with a slightly glandular texture and pleasant scent, arising from slender, creeping rhizomes that spread slowly to form loose colonies. It is closely related to G. dryopteris (common oak fern) but differs in its glandular fronds, preference for calcareous rock, and slightly larger stature. An unusual and choice fern for the rock garden or shaded limestone garden wall; combine with Cystopteris bulbifera and Polypodium virginianum on limestone outcrops.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring wedged into crevices of limestone or other calcareous rock in partial shade to full shade; black-stemmed spleenwort grows in nature in the crevices of shaded limestone cliffs and canyon walls and does best when planted into the joints of a limestone rock wall or rock garden rather than in open soil.

directions: Plant divisions in spring by wedging them into crevices of limestone or calcareous rock in partial to full shade; black-stemmed spleenwort grows naturally in the shaded joints of limestone and dolomite cliffs throughout the southeastern and south-central US and is best established directly in rock crevices rather than in open soil. Asplenium resiliens is a small, tufted evergreen fern with shiny, dark-brown to black stipes (stems) — the feature that gives it its common name — and once-pinnate, 4–10 inch fronds with rounded, alternate pinnae with slightly toothed margins. It is a limestone specialist very similar to the ebony spleenwort (A. platyneuron) but smaller and more heat-tolerant, native to shaded limestone outcrops and canyon walls from Virginia to Arizona. An excellent fern for a shaded limestone rock wall or naturalistic limestone garden, where it will establish in crevices and require essentially no maintenance once settled.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy lip fern thrives in exposed, dry, acidic rock outcrops and open woodland slopes and is among the most drought-tolerant eastern native ferns.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy lip fern is among the most drought-tolerant of the eastern native ferns and thrives in exposed, dry, acidic rock outcrops, talus slopes, and open rocky woodland that would kill more shade-sensitive fern species. Cheilanthes lanosa is a small, tufted, evergreen fern of eastern North America distinctive for its densely hairy fronds — both the stipes and the undersides of the pinnae are covered in rust-colored hairs that give the plant a fuzzy, russet-brown appearance especially in dry conditions — a feature that reflects its adaptation to drought by reducing water loss. It grows naturally on dry, often south-facing, acidic sandstone, quartzite, and occasionally limestone outcrops from Connecticut to Georgia and westward, forming small, persistent colonies. An excellent fern for dry-shade rock gardens, open stone walls, and naturalistic xeric native plantings where its unusual texture and drought tolerance are assets.
